Why are you interested in this role?: What pulls me to this role is the part of implementation people often underestimate—adoption. I learned this the hard way: at InGenius Prep I launched a new sales process with a full document library and assumed people would use it. They didn't. I went back, interviewed the users, found the real blocker was friction not resistance, and built a one-pager that fixed it. That experience reshaped how I work—I now diagnose before I design and treat go-live as the start, not the finish. An Enterprise Implementation Consultant role is that discipline made into a job, and doing it for high-stakes enterprise customers is exactly the challenge I want.
Why do you want to work at Brex?: Two reasons. First, the work fits how I'm wired—I'm a strategic program manager who scopes outcomes, builds the plan, and stays close to stakeholders until the change actually sticks. That's what I do across 20+ programs at InGenius and what I did optimizing onboarding across three global locations at Vestas. Second, Brex sits at the intersection of finance and technology, and I genuinely enjoy translating complex systems into something a customer can adopt and trust. I'd be excited to be the person who turns a Brex launch into measurable value for the customer.
How do you handle ambiguity in a complex: I thrive in it—my consulting work is ambiguous by design. I walk into orgs with open questions and leave with frameworks. My approach is to diagnose before designing: I run discovery, ask questions before assuming, and define what success actually looks like in measurable terms. Then I build in feedback loops so course-correction is cheap. At ODN I ran stakeholder interviews and a 300+ case audit to surface the real cost driver before anyone proposed a solution. That habit—anchoring on data and stakeholder reality first—is what keeps a complex, multi-workstream implementation from drifting.
Tell us about yourself / anything else: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC. In practice that means I diagnose with data where a rollout breaks down, collaborate cross-functionally on the fix, and track results to make sure it holds. I've worked across the US, Scandinavia, and Taiwan—including an office of 22 nationalities—so multi-threaded, cross-cultural stakeholder relationships are familiar territory. I'd love to bring that combination of structure and people-focus to your enterprise customers.
